title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
This page reports the results of a controlled benchmarking experiment comparing query performance between two approaches to storing and querying the same data: a native DuckDB database and direct Parquet files queried via read_parquet(). Both formats contain identical data drawn from three California city subsets of the Stanford Open Policing Project — Los Angeles, San Francisco, and San Jose — sourced from the same MySQL database. The only difference between the two approaches is the storage format itself.
For background on Parquet’s internal structure and how DuckDB reads files directly, see the Parquet & DuckDB reference page.
Six query types are benchmarked to represent a range of workloads, from simple counts to cross-table aggregations:







Query
Description




Q1
COUNT(*) — total row count across a single city


Q2
GROUP BY subject_race — aggregation on one column, single city


Q3
GROUP BY type — aggregation on a different column, single city


Q4
String filter — ILIKE pattern match on a string column


Q5
Cross-table GROUP BY subject_race — aggregation across all three cities



Each timing result is the mean of 20 timed runs, preceded by 3 warmup runs. A fresh DuckDB connection is opened and closed for every trial to avoid connection-level caching effects.


Differences between native DuckDB and direct Parquet are tested using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test at α = 0.05. This non-parametric test is appropriate here because query times are non-negative, right-skewed, and not guaranteed to follow a normal distribution. A result marked Yes* indicates the observed difference in timing distributions is statistically unlikely to have occurred by chance, and we can conclude the two approaches differ meaningfully in performance for that query. A result marked No means the difference is not statistically distinguishable from random variation.

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
This page reports the results of a controlled benchmarking experiment comparing query performance between two approaches to storing and querying the same data: a native DuckDB database and direct Parquet files queried via read_parquet(). Both formats contain identical data drawn from three California city subsets of the Stanford Open Policing Project — Los Angeles, San Francisco, and San Jose — sourced from the same MySQL database. Query
Description




Q1

GROUP BY type — aggregation on a different column, single city

A result marked Yes* indicates the observed difference in timing distributions is statistically unlikely to have occurred by chance, and we can conclude the two approaches differ meaningfully in performance for that query. A result marked No means the difference is not statistically distinguishable from random variation.

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Setup


Show code
library(arrow)
library(duckdb)
library(DBI)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(RMySQL)

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Step 1: Build Storage Formats
This step can be done in many approaches. For this guide, a connection with MySQL is setup, then three tables from the entire SQL database are used to write to its own Parquet file. Then a native DuckDB database is built with each city (Parquet file) as its own table. This only needs to run once so set eval: false after the databases are built.

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Step 2: Storage Size Comparison
One concrete advantage of direct Parquet over native DuckDB is storage footprint and size. Because Parquet files are written with columnar compression applied at the column level, they are typically substantially smaller than an equivalent DuckDB database, which stores data in its own internal format optimized for query performance rather than disk compactness.


Show code
parquet_files <- list.files(here::here("parquet_cities"),
                             pattern = "\\.parquet$", full.names = TRUE)
parquet_size_mb <- round(sum(file.size(parquet_files)) / 1e6, 2)
parquet_size_gb <- round(parquet_size_mb / 1000, 3)

duckdb_size_mb <- round(file.size(here::here("traffic_native.duckdb")) / 1e6, 2)
duckdb_size_gb <- round(duckdb_size_mb / 1000, 3)

ratio <- round(duckdb_size_mb / parquet_size_mb, 2)

size_df <- data.frame(
  Format        = c("Direct Parquet (3 files)", "Native DuckDB (1 file)"),
  `Size (MB)`   = c(parquet_size_mb, duckdb_size_mb),
  `Size (GB)`   = c(parquet_size_gb, duckdb_size_gb),
  `Files`       = c(3, 1),
  `Ratio vs Parquet` = c(1.00, ratio)
)

knitr::kable(size_df, align = "r",
  caption = "Storage size comparison: direct Parquet vs native DuckDB")



Storage size comparison: direct Parquet vs native DuckDB









Format
Size..MB.
Size..GB.
Files
Ratio.vs.Parquet




Direct Parquet (3 files)
90.24
0.090
3
1.00


Native DuckDB (1 file)
83.64
0.084
1
0.93





Beyond storage size, direct Parquet also avoids the ingestion step entirely. The data can be queried immediately after the Parquet files are written, with no loading or database construction required. The trade-off is query performance, which the remainder of this page explores.

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Step 3: Benchmark
This section contains the code for creating the queries and obtaining the mean times for each query. In this code, 5 queries were performed and are meant to represent varying forms of queries, from counting rows to cross-table queries.


Show code
la_parquet <- here::here("parquet_cities", "la.parquet")
sf_parquet <- here::here("parquet_cities", "sf.parquet")
sj_parquet <- here::here("parquet_cities", "sj.parquet")

queries <- list(

  Q1 = list(
    label  = "COUNT(*) — LA only",
    direct = sprintf("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM read_parquet('%s')", la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM la"
  ),

  Q2 = list(
    label  = "GROUP BY subject_race — LA only",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM read_parquet('%s') GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC",
      la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM la GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC"
  ),

  Q3 = list(
    label  = "GROUP BY type — LA only",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT type, COUNT(*) AS n FROM read_parquet('%s') GROUP BY type ORDER BY n DESC",
      la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT type, COUNT(*) AS n FROM la GROUP BY type ORDER BY n DESC"
  ),

  Q4 = list(
    label  = "String filter ILIKE — LA only",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM read_parquet('%s') WHERE raw_descent_description ILIKE '%%hispanic%%'",
      la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM la WHERE raw_descent_description ILIKE '%hispanic%'"
  ),

  Q5 = list(
    label  = "Cross-table GROUP BY subject_race — all three cities",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM (
         SELECT subject_race FROM read_parquet('%s')
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM read_parquet('%s')
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM read_parquet('%s')
       ) GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC",
      la_parquet, sf_parquet, sj_parquet),
    native = "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM (
         SELECT subject_race FROM la
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM sf
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM sj
       ) GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC"
  )
)




Show code
fresh_direct <- function() dbConnect(duckdb())
fresh_native <- function() dbConnect(duckdb(), here::here("traffic_native.duckdb"))

n_warmup <- 3
n_trials <- 20

run_trials <- function(con_fn, sql, n_warmup, n_trials) {
  for (i in seq_len(n_warmup)) {
    con <- con_fn()
    try(dbGetQuery(con, sql), silent = TRUE)
    try(dbDisconnect(con, shutdown = TRUE), silent = TRUE)
    gc()
  }
  times_ms <- numeric(n_trials)
  for (i in seq_len(n_trials)) {
    con <- con_fn()
    tryCatch({
      times_ms[i] <- system.time(dbGetQuery(con, sql))["elapsed"] * 1000
    }, error = function(e) {
      times_ms[i] <<- NA
    }, finally = {
      try(dbDisconnect(con, shutdown = TRUE), silent = TRUE)
    })
    gc()
  }
  times_ms
}

all_trials <- data.frame(
  query_id   = character(),
  label      = character(),
  format     = character(),
  trial      = integer(),
  time_ms    = numeric()
)

for (qid in names(queries)) {
  q <- queries[[qid]]
  cat("\nRunning", qid, "—", q$label, "\n")

  cat("  Native...\n")
  nt <- run_trials(fresh_native, q$native, n_warmup, n_trials)
  cat("  Native mean:", round(mean(nt, na.rm = TRUE), 1), "ms\n")

  cat("  Direct...\n")
  dt <- run_trials(fresh_direct, q$direct, n_warmup, n_trials)
  cat("  Direct mean:", round(mean(dt, na.rm = TRUE), 1), "ms\n")

  all_trials <- rbind(all_trials,
    data.frame(query_id = qid, label = q$label, format = "native",
               trial = seq_len(n_trials), time_ms = round(nt, 3)),
    data.frame(query_id = qid, label = q$label, format = "direct",
               trial = seq_len(n_trials), time_ms = round(dt, 3))
  )
}



Running Q1 — COUNT(*) — LA only 
  Native...


  Native mean: NaN ms
  Direct...
  Direct mean: 1 ms

Running Q2 — GROUP BY subject_race — LA only 
  Native...


  Native mean: NaN ms
  Direct...
  Direct mean: 9.2 ms

Running Q3 — GROUP BY type — LA only 
  Native...


  Native mean: NaN ms
  Direct...
  Direct mean: 10 ms

Running Q4 — String filter ILIKE — LA only 
  Native...


  Native mean: NaN ms
  Direct...
  Direct mean: 24 ms

Running Q5 — Cross-table GROUP BY subject_race — all three cities 
  Native...


  Native mean: NaN ms
  Direct...
  Direct mean: 10.9 ms

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Results

Summary Table


Show code
summary_df <- all_trials %>%
  group_by(query_id, label, format) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_ms = round(mean(time_ms, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = format, values_from = mean_ms) %>%
  mutate(
    diff_ms = round(direct - native, 1),
    ratio   = round(direct / native, 2),
    faster  = ifelse(native < direct, "Native", "Direct Parquet")
  )

# Add significance test
sig_col <- mapply(function(qid) {
  nt <- all_trials %>% filter(query_id == qid, format == "native") %>% pull(time_ms)
  dt <- all_trials %>% filter(query_id == qid, format == "direct") %>% pull(time_ms)
  p  <- tryCatch(
    wilcox.test(nt, dt, exact = FALSE)$p.value,
    error = function(e) NA
  )
  ifelse(!is.na(p) & p < 0.05, "Yes *", "No")
}, summary_df$query_id)

summary_df$significant <- sig_col

knitr::kable(
  summary_df %>%
    select(label, native, direct, diff_ms, ratio, faster, significant),
  col.names = c("Query", "Native (ms)", "Direct (ms)",
                "Diff (ms)", "Ratio (D/N)", "Faster", "Sig. (p < 0.05)"),
  align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "l", "c"),
  caption = "Mean query time: native DuckDB vs direct Parquet. Ratio > 1 means direct Parquet is slower. Significance assessed via Wilcoxon rank-sum test."
)



Mean query time: native DuckDB vs direct Parquet. Ratio > 1 means direct Parquet is slower. Significance assessed via Wilcoxon rank-sum test.











Query
Native (ms)
Direct (ms)
Diff (ms)
Ratio (D/N)
Faster
Sig. (p < 0.05)




COUNT(*) — LA only
NaN
1.0
NaN
NaN
NA
No


GROUP BY subject_race — LA only
NaN
9.2
NaN
NaN
NA
No


GROUP BY type — LA only
NaN
10.1
NaN
NaN
NA
No


String filter ILIKE — LA only
NaN
24.0
NaN
NaN
NA
No


Cross-table GROUP BY subject_race — all three cities
NaN
10.9
NaN
NaN
NA
No







Visual Representation
The chart below shows the direct/native ratio for each query type. A ratio above 1 (dashed line) means direct Parquet was slower than native DuckDB for that query; below 1 means direct Parquet was faster.


Show code
plot_df <- summary_df %>%
  mutate(
    label     = factor(label, levels = rev(label)),
    color_grp = ifelse(ratio > 1, "Native faster", "Direct faster")
  )

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = ratio, y = label, colour = color_grp)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#94a3b8", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1, xend = ratio, y = label, yend = label),
               linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f×", ratio)),
            hjust = ifelse(plot_df$ratio > 1, -0.3, 1.3),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Native faster" = "#185FA5",
                                 "Direct faster" = "#0F6E56")) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(
      min(0.5, min(plot_df$ratio) * 0.85),
      max(1.5, max(plot_df$ratio) * 1.15)
    ),
    breaks = seq(0, 10, by = 0.5)
  ) +
  labs(
    x      = "Ratio (Direct / Native)",
    y      = NULL,
    colour = NULL,
    title  = "Direct Parquet vs Native DuckDB: Query Time Ratio",
    subtitle = "Dashed line at 1.0 = equal performance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "top",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )





Direct-to-native query time ratio by query type. Ratio > 1 indicates native DuckDB is faster; ratio < 1 indicates direct Parquet is faster.

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Conclusion
The results show a distinguishable difference that native DuckDB offers faster and more consistent query performance across all of the query types. Direct Parquet offers the advantage that files can be queried immediately after writing and occupy significantly less disk space.
The results can be understood based on the knowledge of how DuckDB querying and direct Parquet querying functions. When data is ingested into a native DuckDB database, DuckDB builds and maintains its own internal columnar storage format, statistics, and metadata that are optimized specifically for its query engine. When a query runs against a native table, DuckDB can exploit these internals directly, for example, reading only the pages it needs, using pre-computed column statistics, and operating entirely within a single file with no coordination overhead. Direct Parquet querying, by contrast, requires DuckDB to open each file, parse its footer, decompress column chunks, and assemble results from separate sources on every query execution. Even though Parquet is also columnar and compressed, this per-query parsing and file-handling overhead adds time that the native format avoids entirely.
For workflows involving repeated analytical queries over stable data, native DuckDB is the stronger choice. For workflows where storage efficiency, portability, or avoiding ingestion latency matters more than raw query speed, direct Parquet is competitive and in some cases faster.

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
When you write a multi-table JOIN, you specify which tables connect and on what columns — but you do not specify how the database should physically combine them, nor in what order. Those two decisions belong to the query planner, and they are the single largest lever on how fast a join runs.
This demo isolates and measures two planner decisions:

Join strategy — the physical algorithm used to match rows between two tables. PostgreSQL has three: Nested Loop, Hash Join, and Merge Join.
Join order — the sequence in which tables are brought into the join. Starting from heavily-filtered small tables keeps intermediate results tiny; starting from large unfiltered tables forces the engine to carry millions of rows through every step.

Normally the planner picks both automatically using table statistics and cost estimates. To see the effect of each choice, we override the planner with session-level SET commands, force one strategy and one order at a time, and time the result. This is purely a teaching device — in production you almost always let the planner decide.
We use the Stack Exchange stats dataset (the Cross Validated dump), which has four tables we can chain together: votes, posts, users, and badges.


We connect to the database, build indexes on the join columns, and confirm the connection with a trivial query. Indexes matter here: a Nested Loop join is only competitive when the inner side can be probed through an index rather than scanned in full.


Show the code
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)
library(purrr)
library(tidyverse)

con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = "stats",
  host   = "127.0.0.1",
  port   = 5432,
  user   = Sys.info()[["user"]]
)

# Index the columns that appear in our JOIN ... ON clauses
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_votes_postid       ON votes(postid)")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_posts_owneruserid  ON posts(owneruserid)")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_badges_userid      ON badges(userid)")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbGetQuery(con, "EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT COUNT(*) FROM votes")

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
When you write a multi-table JOIN, you specify which tables connect and on what columns — but you do not specify how the database should physically combine them, nor in what order. Those two decisions belong to the query planner, and they are the single largest lever on how fast a join runs.
This demo isolates and measures two planner decisions:

Join strategy — the physical algorithm used to match rows between two tables. PostgreSQL has three: Nested Loop, Hash Join, and Merge Join.
Join order — the sequence in which tables are brought into the join. Starting from heavily-filtered small tables keeps intermediate results tiny; starting from large unfiltered tables forces the engine to carry millions of rows through every step.

Normally the planner picks both automatically using table statistics and cost estimates. To see the effect of each choice, we override the planner with session-level SET commands, force one strategy and one order at a time, and time the result. This is purely a teaching device — in production you almost always let the planner decide.
We use the Stack Exchange stats dataset (the Cross Validated dump), which has four tables we can chain together: votes, posts, users, and badges.


We connect to the database, build indexes on the join columns, and confirm the connection with a trivial query. Indexes matter here: a Nested Loop join is only competitive when the inner side can be probed through an index rather than scanned in full.


Show the code
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)
library(purrr)
library(tidyverse)

con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = "stats",
  host   = "127.0.0.1",
  port   = 5432,
  user   = Sys.info()[["user"]]
)

# Index the columns that appear in our JOIN ... ON clauses
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_votes_postid       ON votes(postid)")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_posts_owneruserid  ON posts(owneruserid)")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_badges_userid      ON badges(userid)")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbGetQuery(con, "EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT COUNT(*) FROM votes")

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
section: How the planner can be controlled
text:
How the planner can be controlled
PostgreSQL exposes a set of session variables that let us switch individual planner behaviours on and off. These are the knobs we use throughout:

enable_nestloop, enable_hashjoin, enable_mergejoin — turn each physical strategy on or off. By enabling exactly one at a time, we force the planner to use that strategy.
join_collapse_limit / from_collapse_limit — when set to 1, the planner is forbidden from reordering the tables and must join them in the literal order written in the FROM clause. This is what lets us dictate join order.
max_parallel_workers_per_gather — set to 0 to turn off parallelism, so timing differences reflect the algorithm and not the number of workers.

A key idea: because join_collapse_limit = 1 makes the engine respect the written order, the same logical query written with its tables in a different sequence becomes a different physical plan.


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET join_collapse_limit = 1")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET from_collapse_limit = 1")


[1] 0

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
section: The two queries: good order vs. bad order
text:
The two queries: good order vs. bad order
Both queries below answer the exact same question — count the rows produced by joining votes to their posts, those posts to their owners, and those owners to their badges, keeping only upvotes (votetypeid = 2) on well-scored posts (score > 10) owned by high-reputation users (reputation > 1000). They return identical results. The only difference is the order the tables are listed in.

Good order leads with votes and posts, where the WHERE filters are most selective, so the very first join already works on a shrunken set of rows.
Bad order leads with badges and users, the large tables whose filters don’t bite until later, so early intermediate results stay huge.



Show the code
# Good order: filtered/small tables first
sql_good_order <- "
  SELECT COUNT(*)
  FROM votes v
  JOIN posts  p ON v.postid = p.id
  JOIN users  u ON p.owneruserid = u.id
  JOIN badges b ON b.userid = u.id
  WHERE v.votetypeid = 2
    AND p.score > 10
    AND u.reputation > 1000
"

# Bad order: large/unfiltered tables first
sql_bad_order <- "
  SELECT COUNT(*)
  FROM badges b
  JOIN users  u ON b.userid = u.id
  JOIN posts  p ON p.owneruserid = u.id
  JOIN votes  v ON v.postid = p.id
  WHERE v.votetypeid = 2
    AND p.score > 10
    AND u.reputation > 1000
"

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
A repeatable timing harness (warm cache)
To get a stable measurement we wrap EXPLAIN ANALYZE in a small helper. It runs one throwaway query first to load the relevant data pages into PostgreSQL’s buffer cache (a warm-cache measurement), then runs the query n more times, scraping the Execution Time and Planning Time lines out of each plan.
EXPLAIN ANALYZE is the tool doing the real work here: it actually executes the query and reports how long each step took, as opposed to plain EXPLAIN, which only prints the planner’s estimate.


Show the code
run_repeated_warm <- function(con, query, n = 10) {

  # Warm-up run: loads pages into cache, result discarded
  dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", query))

  # Timed trials
  map(1:n, function(i) {
    lines <- dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", query))[[1]]

    exec_time <- lines[grep("Execution Time", lines)] |>
      str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()

    plan_time <- lines[grep("Planning Time", lines)] |>
      str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()

    tibble(run = i, exec_ms = exec_time, plan_ms = plan_time)
  }) |>
  list_rbind()
}



Running all six combinations
We now cross the two join orders with the three join strategies, forcing one strategy at a time by toggling the enable_* flags. That gives six timing distributions of ten runs each.


Show the code
# --- Nested Loop ---
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop  = on")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin  = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
results_nest_good <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Nested Loop", order = "good")
results_nest_bad  <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Nested Loop", order = "bad")

# --- Hash Join ---
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop  = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin  = on")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
results_hash_good <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Hash Join", order = "good")
results_hash_bad  <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Hash Join", order = "bad")

# --- Merge Join ---
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop  = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin  = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = on")


[1] 0


Show the code
results_merge_good <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Merge Join", order = "good")
results_merge_bad  <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Merge Join", order = "bad")




Show the code
bind_rows(
  results_nest_good, results_nest_bad,
  results_hash_good, results_hash_bad,
  results_merge_good, results_merge_bad
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = join_type, y = exec_ms, color = order)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title    = "Effect of join strategy on execution time",
    subtitle = "4-table join: votes, posts, users, badges",
    x        = "Join Strategy",
    y        = "Execution time (ms)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





Warm-cache execution time across the six strategy × order combinations.




What the plot shows. Each strategy responds differently to a bad join order, and that is the whole point. A Nested Loop is the most order-sensitive: for every row from the outer side it probes the inner side, so if the outer side starts large (bad order) the probe count explodes. A Hash Join builds a hash table on one input and streams the other past it, which makes it far more forgiving of a bad starting order. A Merge Join requires both inputs sorted on the join key, so its cost is dominated by sorting and it sits somewhere in between. In short: a good join order helps every strategy, but it rescues the Nested Loop.

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
section: Sanity check: the two orders return the same answer
text:
Sanity check: the two orders return the same answer
Before trusting the timings, we confirm that “good” and “bad” really are the same query logically — same count — and we list the indexes currently in place.


Show the code
dbGetQuery(con, sql_good_order)



  



Show the code
dbGetQuery(con, sql_bad_order)



  



Show the code
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT tablename, indexname
  FROM pg_indexes
  WHERE schemaname = 'public'
  ORDER BY tablename
")

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
section: Does it hold up with a cold cache?
text:
Does it hold up with a cold cache?
Warm-cache numbers reward queries whose pages already sit in memory. A cold-cache test is harsher and closer to a first-of-the-day query. We can’t flush the OS page cache without sudo, but we can approximate a cold session by reconnecting fresh each iteration and issuing DISCARD ALL, which resets all session state.


Show the code
run_repeated_cold <- function(con, multi_join, n = 10) {
  map(1:n, function(i) {

    # Fresh connection each iteration
    con <- dbConnect(
      RPostgres::Postgres(),
      dbname = "stats",
      host   = "127.0.0.1",
      port   = 5432,
      user   = Sys.info()[["user"]]
    )

    dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")   # clear session cache (no sudo needed)
    Sys.sleep(0.5)

    lines <- dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", multi_join))[[1]]

    exec_time <- lines[grep("Execution Time", lines)] |>
      str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()
    plan_time <- lines[grep("Planning Time", lines)] |>
      str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()

    dbDisconnect(con)
    tibble(run = i, exec_ms = exec_time, plan_ms = plan_time)
  }) |>
  list_rbind()
}


Because each cold iteration opens its own connection, the per-strategy SET flags must be re-established on the main connection before each batch, and we issue DISCARD ALL between batches to keep them independent.


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET join_collapse_limit = 1")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET from_collapse_limit = 1")


[1] 0


Show the code
# --- Nested Loop ---
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop = on")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")


[1] 0


Show the code
cold_nest_good <- run_repeated_cold(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Nested Loop", order = "good")
dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")


[1] 0


Show the code
cold_nest_bad  <- run_repeated_cold(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Nested Loop", order = "bad")

# --- Hash Join ---
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin = on")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")


[1] 0


Show the code
cold_hash_good <- run_repeated_cold(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Hash Join", order = "good")
dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")


[1] 0


Show the code
cold_hash_bad  <- run_repeated_cold(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Hash Join", order = "bad")

# --- Merge Join ---
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin = off")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = on")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")


[1] 0


Show the code
cold_merge_good <- run_repeated_cold(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Merge Join", order = "good")
dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")


[1] 0


Show the code
cold_merge_bad  <- run_repeated_cold(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Merge Join", order = "bad")




Show the code
bind_rows(
  cold_nest_good, cold_nest_bad,
  cold_hash_good, cold_hash_bad,
  cold_merge_good, cold_merge_bad
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = join_type, y = exec_ms, color = order)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title    = "Effect of join strategy on execution time (cold cache)",
    subtitle = "4-table join: votes, posts, users, badges",
    x        = "Join Strategy",
    y        = "Execution time (ms)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





Cold-cache execution time. The order penalty typically widens when pages must be fetched from disk.

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
section: Why the planner makes these choices: estimated vs. actual rows
text:
Why the planner makes these choices: estimated vs. actual rows
The planner doesn’t time queries — it estimates how many rows each step will emit and picks the cheapest plan based on those estimates. When the estimates are wrong, the plan is wrong. The function below pulls every node out of a plan and compares the planner’s estimated row count to the actual row count, computing a q-error: the ratio of the larger to the smaller. A q-error near 1 means the estimate was accurate; a large q-error means the planner was badly misled at that node.


Show the code
extract_estimates <- function(con, sql, label) {
  plan <- dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", sql))[[1]]

  # Keep only nodes that report actual timing
  node_lines <- plan[grep("actual time", plan)]

  map(node_lines, function(line) {
    operation <- str_extract(line,
      "(Nested Loop|Hash Join|Merge Join|Seq Scan|Index Only Scan|Index Scan|Aggregate|Gather)")

    estimated <- str_extract(line, "rows=([0-9]+)\\s+width") |>
      str_extract("[0-9]+") |> as.numeric()

    actual <- str_extract(line, "actual time=[0-9.]+\\.\\.[0-9.]+ rows=([0-9]+)") |>
      str_extract("[0-9]+$") |> as.numeric()

    loops <- str_extract(line, "loops=([0-9]+)") |>
      str_extract("[0-9]+") |> as.numeric()

    tibble(
      label        = label,
      operation    = operation,
      estimated    = estimated,
      actual       = actual,
      loops        = loops,
      actual_total = actual * loops,
      q_error      = round(pmax(estimated / actual, actual / estimated), 1)
    )
  }) |>
  list_rbind() |>
  filter(!is.na(operation), !is.na(estimated), !is.na(actual))
}




Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0")


[1] 0


Show the code
# Forced orders: planner not allowed to reorder
dbExecute(con, "SET join_collapse_limit = 1")


[1] 0


Show the code
good_estimates <- extract_estimates(con, sql_good_order, "forced good order")
bad_estimates  <- extract_estimates(con, sql_bad_order,  "forced bad order")

# Auto-optimized: planner free to choose its own order
dbExecute(con, "SET join_collapse_limit = 8")


[1] 0


Show the code
auto_estimates <- extract_estimates(con, sql_good_order, "auto-optimized")

# Reset session to normal behaviour
dbExecute(con, "SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 4")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbExecute(con, "SET join_collapse_limit = 8")


[1] 0


Show the code
all_estimates <- bind_rows(good_estimates, bad_estimates, auto_estimates)
all_estimates



  



Reading this table. The auto-optimized rows show what the planner does when left alone (join_collapse_limit = 8 lets it reorder up to eight tables freely). Comparing q-errors across the three labels shows where estimation breaks down: nodes deep in the bad-order plan tend to carry the largest q-errors, because each early misestimate compounds as it feeds the next join. This is the mechanism behind the timing differences — a bad order isn’t slow by fiat, it’s slow because the planner’s row estimates drift further from reality at every step, and the actual_total (rows × loops) balloons.


Show the code
dbDisconnect(con)

title: Join Strategies and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
Summary










Nested Loop
Hash Join
Merge Join




How it matches rows
Probe inner table once per outer row
Build hash on one side, stream other
Sort both sides, walk in lockstep


Needs an index?
Strongly benefits from inner index
No
Benefits from pre-sorted inputs


Sensitivity to join order
Very high
Low
Moderate


Best when
Outer side is small after filtering
Inputs are large and unsorted
Inputs already sorted on the key



Two takeaways. First, join order matters most for Nested Loops: leading with your most-filtered tables keeps the outer row count — and therefore the probe count — small. Second, the planner’s choices flow directly from its row estimates, so accurate statistics (and thus low q-error) are what let it pick the right strategy and order on its own. Forcing these settings is useful for understanding the engine, but in practice the lesson is to keep statistics fresh and write selective filters, then trust the planner.

title: SQL Joins
text:
● Relational Query Operations
    SQLJoins
    What is a join? How do logical join types differ from the physical strategies the optimizer actually runs — nested loops, sort-merge, and hash?
    
      Join Keys
      Logical Joins
      Physical Strategies
      Optimizer Choices
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    What are joins & join keys?
    Joins are key operations in relational database querying that let practitioners extract information from multiple tables simultaneously. Most joins are executed using a key shared by both tables — keys whose construction matches so they can correctly identify matches between tables.
  





  
  

    
    
      
        Two tables joined on a shared key
        
          
            
              departments
              KEYdept
              dname
            
            
              dept
              ↔
              links
            
            
              buildings
              KEYdept
              building
            
          
          



dept
dname
building




2
Art
STAR


5
Math
Estella




          The dept column exists in both tables. A join matches rows where the two keys are equal, combining their columns into one result.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Logical join types · A (m rows), B (n rows)
        
          
            
              
              
              
            
            Inner JoinOnly rows where A's key matches B's. A ∩ B
          
          
            
              
              
            
            Full Outer JoinMatched and unmatched rows from both, nulls for misses. A ∪ B · returns m + n
          
          
            
              
              
              
            
            Left / Right Outer JoinAll rows from one side, matches from the other. A ∪ (A ∩ B) · at most m + n
          
          
            
              
              ×
              
            
            Cross JoinEvery combination — the Cartesian product. A × B · returns m × n
          
        
      
    

  

  
  

    
      1.1 · Join Basics
      Joins combine rows across tables
      A join lets you query multiple tables at once, pulling related information into a single result set. Rather than duplicating data, relational databases keep it spread across tables and stitch it together at query time.
      Joins are most commonly executed using a key from both involved tables. These keys are often identical in their construction so that they can correctly identify matches between tables.
      To learn more about keys and their structure — primary keys, foreign keys, and the indexes that make them fast — see the indexes and B-trees page.
      
        Multiple tables
        Shared key
        No duplication
      
    

    
      1.2 · Logical Join Types
      The four logical joins you can write
      Given two tables, A (m rows) and B (n rows), the logical join type defines what the result should contain:
      An inner join returns only rows where A's join key matches B's — analogous to A ∩ B. A full outer join returns matching and unmatched rows from both tables, denoting missing matches as nulls — analogous to A ∪ B, returning exactly m + n rows.
      A left or right outer join is like a full outer join but keeps unmatched rows from only one side — analogous to A ∪ (A ∩ B), returning at most m + n rows. A cross join takes the full Cartesian product, every possible combination of rows — analogous to A × B, returning m × n rows.
      
        Inner · A ∩ B
        Full outer · A ∪ B
        Left / Right
        Cross · A × B
      
    

  




  
    Two layers, one query
    The logical join says what you want. The physical join decides how the engine gets it.
    Understanding both layers helps you write more efficient queries, reduce computation costs, and diagnose performance bottlenecks. The same logical join can run with radically different speed depending on the physical strategy the optimizer picks.
  



  02
  
    Under the Hood
    Logical vs. physical joins
    Independently of which logical join you write, the query optimizer decides how to execute it. Queries pass through logical query plans that must be physically implemented, and several physical join types may be selected depending on your engine. The three main strategies are nested loops, sort-merge, and hash.
  





  

    
    
      
        From SQL text to executed join
        
          
            SELECT * FROM A JOIN B  ON A.dept = B.dept;
          
          ↓ logical join (what)
          Optimizer builds & compares query plans
          ↓ physical join (how)
          
            NestedLoop
            Sort-Merge
            Hash
          
          PostgreSQL allows the most granular control over physical join execution settings. Knowing which join the optimizer uses is essential to improving a query's efficiency.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · Two Levels of a Join
      What you write vs. how it runs
      When executing a SQL query, joins operate on two distinct levels: the logical join (what you define in SQL syntax) and the physical join (how the query optimizer actually executes it behind the scenes).
      When queries run, they go through a process of creating logical query plans that eventually need to be physically implemented. Several physical join types may be selected during this implementation depending on your query engine.
      PostgreSQL allows for the most granular control over physical join execution settings. The three main physical join strategies are nested loops, merge, and hash — and understanding which one the optimizer uses is essential to improving the query's efficiency.
      
        Logical = what
        Physical = how
        Query plans
      
    

  




  03
  
    Physical Strategies
    The three physical joins
    Nested loops, sort-merge, and hash. Each has a distinct algorithm, cost profile, and sweet spot. The animations below walk through exactly how each one matches rows and assembles the result.
  





  

    
    
      
        Nested loop · scan inner for each outer row
        
          
        
        The outer row (green) stays fixed while the engine scans the entire inner table for matching keys, appending each match to the result.
      
    

    
    
      
        Nested loop on a unique key · search stops early
        
          
        
        When the inner key is unique (a primary key), the scan ends as soon as the single match is found — no need to scan the rest of the inner table.
      
    

    
    
      
        Why nested loops can be slow
        
          Worst case · unsorted inputs
          O(m × n)
          every outer row scans the whole inner table
        
        
          ✓Index or sort the inner table so the scan ends early once all matching keys are found.
          ✓Join on a primary key and the search ends after one match, since primary keys are unique by definition.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · Nested Loop Join
      The simplest strategy: scan for every row
      The nested loop join is the simplest physical strategy to understand. For simplicity we focus on two-table inner joins, and assume neither table is sorted.
      The smaller table is designated the outer table, the larger the inner. Staying at a fixed row in the outer table, the engine performs a sequential scan on the inner table until it finds a match, records that match, moves to the next outer row, scans the entire inner table again, and so on.
      
        Outer × inner
        Sequential scan
        Simplest to grasp
      
    

    
      3.2 · Unique-Key Shortcut
      A unique inner key ends the search early
      You can improve performance dramatically by indexing or sorting the inner table, so the engine no longer has to scan the entire inner table on each pass — the search can end early once all instances of the join key are found.
      Additionally, if you're joining on primary keys, the search ends once a single matching instance is found, since primary keys must be unique by definition. The animation shows the scan halting at the first (and only) match.
      
        Early stop
        Unique key
        Indexed inner
      
    

    
      3.3 · Cost Profile
      Catastrophic on large unsorted inputs
      This is why nested loop joins can cause catastrophically long runtimes with unsorted input tables. If table A has m rows and table B has n rows, the worst-case time complexity is O(m × n).
      That said, nested loops require the least overhead, so they may outperform the other strategies on small datasets or when a good index exists on the inner table — exactly the cases shown in the two animations above.
      
        O(m × n) worst case
        Great when small
        Low overhead
      
    

  



  
    From quadratic to linear
    Nested loops can cost m × n. Sort-merge and hash read each table about once — cost proportional to m + n.
    The next two strategies trade a little setup work — sorting, or building a hash table — for a dramatically better cost profile on large inputs.
  





  

    
    
      
        Sort-merge · phase 1: sort each input
        
          



dept
dname




2
Art


5
Math


7
Music




          



dept
building




2
STAR


5
Estella


7
Thatcher


9
Seaver




        
        Each input is split into memory-sized runs, each run sorted individually (by index or sort algorithm), then written back — both inputs sorted on the join key.
      
    

    
    
      
        Sort-merge · phase 2: merge with two pointers
        
          
        
        A pointer walks each sorted table in the same direction. On a match (teal) the row joins the result; otherwise the pointer at the smaller value advances.
      
    

    
    
      
        Cost: each table read at most once
        
          Merge cost (already sorted)
          O(m + n)
          proportional to the sum, not the product
        
        
          ⚑Requires at least one equijoin predicate — equality on the key must be a join condition.
          ⚠The sort requirement can dominate cost if the data isn't already sorted.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.4 · Merge (Sort-Merge) Join
      Most efficient — if the data is pre-sorted
      A merge join is one of the most efficient joins, assuming the two datasets are pre-sorted. That requirement is also its catch: sorting first can drastically impact overall computation.
      Merge joins require at least one equijoin predicate — equality between key values must be at least one condition of the join. In the sort phase, the dataset is split into runs that fit in memory, each run is sorted individually, and the sorted blocks are written back to disk. Runs can be sorted by indexes or explicit sorting algorithms — indexes are often faster.
      
        Equijoin required
        Sort phase
        Memory-sized runs
      
    

    
      3.5 · The Merge Phase
      Two pointers sweep the sorted runs
      The sorted runs are combined into a final result. The engine maintains a pointer on each sorted dataset and traverses both in the same direction simultaneously.
      As matches are found, they are stored. Once there isn't a match, the pointer at the smallest value advances. This continues until all rows have been processed — exactly the sweep shown in the animation.
      
        Twin pointers
        Advance smaller side
        Single pass
      
    

    
      3.6 · Cost Profile
      Cost scales with the sum, not the product
      A key advantage over nested loops is the cost profile: unlike the nested loop join, where total cost may be proportional to the product of the row counts, with a merge join each table is read at most once and total cost is proportional to the sum of the input row counts.
      It's worth noting that performance may vary with one-to-many or many-to-many relationships, and there is additional nuance in the approach for many-to-many situations.
      
        O(m + n)
        Read once each
        Many-to-many nuance
      
    

  





  

    
    
      
        A hash table: keys → buckets
        
          key
          → hash() →
          bucket #
        
        
          5  Math
          7  Music
          2  Art
        
        A hash table stores key-value pairs in a fixed-size array. A key "hashes" to a specific bucket, so lookups need only hash the key — no scanning.
      
    

    
    
      
        Hash join · build, then probe
        
          
        
        Build (orange): hash each row of the smaller table into a bucket. Probe (teal): hash each row of the larger table and check its bucket for a match.
      
    

    
    
      
        Collisions & cost
        
          Linear in the inputs
          O(N + M)
          no pre-sorting required
        
        
          Hash collision
          Two distinct keys land in the same bucket. The system checks each value in the bucket for a true match. A well-distributed hash function keeps collisions minimal.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.7 · Hash Join
      First, what is a hash table?
      To understand the hash join, it helps to know what a hash table is: a data structure that stores information in key-value pairs for efficient lookups. It's an array of predetermined size, where a small representative piece of information — the key — hashes (corresponds) to a specific index in the array.
      To find a value, you only need to hash the key to extract the relevant information. A hash join relies on this and operates in two distinct phases: the build phase and the probe phase.
      
        Key-value pairs
        Buckets
        Fast lookup
      
    

    
      3.8 · Build & Probe
      Build a table, then probe it
      In the build phase, the typically smaller table (the build input) is scanned and a hash table is constructed using the join-key values. Each entry stores a tuple along with its hash value, distributing data evenly across buckets.
      In the probe phase, the larger table (the probe input) is scanned. Each tuple's join key is hashed with the same function, and that hash points to the bucket to check for matches. If a match is found, tuples from both inputs are combined — as the animation shows.
      
        Build · smaller table
        Probe · larger table
        Same hash function
      
    

    
      3.9 · Collisions & Cost
      Linear cost, watch for collisions
      A hash collision occurs when two or more distinct join-key values hash to the same bucket. The system must then check each value in the bucket to determine a true match. Depending on collision count and bucket size, this can slow the join — but a well-distributed hash function keeps collisions minimal.
      Hash joins are commonly used for larger joins on unsorted data, as the algorithm's time complexity is linear at O(N + M) and it does not require pre-sorted data the way a merge join does.
      
        O(N + M)
        Collisions
        No sorting needed
      
    

  



  04
  
    Decisions
    When does the optimizer choose each?
    Knowing the three strategies is only useful if you know when each one wins. The optimizer weighs data size, sortedness, the join predicate, and available indexes to pick the cheapest plan.
  





  

    
    
      
        Choosing a physical join
        
          
            Nested Loop
            Small datasets, or a good index on the inner table. Least overhead.
          
          
            Sort-Merge
            Both inputs already sorted on the key, or range-based joins. Minimal memory.
          
          
            Hash
            Large, unsorted data with equality joins, especially if one table fits in memory.
          
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Cost at a glance
        
          Nested loop (unsorted)O(m × n)
          
        
        
          Sort-merge (pre-sorted)O(m + n)
          
        
        
          Hash (unsorted, equality)O(N + M)
          
        
        In theory both hash and merge perform well on large tables. The deciding factors are sortedness, the join predicate, memory, and whether a useful index exists.
      
    

  

  

    
      4.1 · Picking a Strategy
      Each strategy has a sweet spot
      In theory, both hash and merge joins perform well with large tables. A query engine typically uses a hash join for large, unsorted datasets and equality joins — especially if one table fits in memory.
      A merge join is often used when both tables are pre-sorted on the join key, or for range-based joins, since it minimizes memory usage and avoids the extra I/O of building a hash table.
      Nested loops, while the least scalable, may outperform both on small datasets or when a good index is available on the inner table, since they require the least overhead.
      
        Hash · big & unsorted
        Merge · pre-sorted / range
        Nested · small / indexed
      
    

    
      4.2 · Cost Comparison
      Sum beats product — when you can get it
      The headline difference is complexity. Nested loops can degrade to O(m × n) on unsorted inputs, while sort-merge and hash both scale with the sum of the inputs once their setup is paid for.
      But complexity isn't the whole story: merge pays a sort cost, hash pays to build a table and risks collisions, and nested loops carry almost no overhead. The optimizer weighs all of this against your actual data and indexes to choose the cheapest plan.
      
        Nested O(m × n)
        Merge O(m + n)
        Hash O(N + M)
      
    

  




  Key Takeaways
  
    
      01
      Joins combine rows from multiple tables, most often by matching a key shared by both. Matching keys are usually identical in construction so they can correctly identify matches.
    
    
      02
      Logical join types define what the result holds: inner (A ∩ B), full outer (A ∪ B, m + n rows), left/right outer (at most m + n), and cross (A × B, m × n rows).
    
    
      03
      Logical vs. physical: whatever logical join you write, the optimizer chooses how to run it — nested loop, sort-merge, or hash — by building and comparing query plans.
    
    
      04
      Nested loop join scans the inner table for every outer row — O(m × n) on unsorted data. Indexing or sorting the inner table, or joining on a unique primary key, lets the search stop early.
    
    
      05
      Sort-merge join sorts both inputs, then sweeps them with twin pointers, advancing the smaller side. Each table is read at most once, so cost is proportional to m + n. Needs an equijoin predicate.
    
    
      06
      Hash join builds a hash table from the smaller input, then probes it with the larger one. Linear O(N + M), no sorting needed; watch for collisions, minimized by a good hash function.
    
    
      07
      Optimizer choice: hash for large unsorted equality joins, merge for pre-sorted or range joins, nested loops for small datasets or a well-indexed inner table.
